title_en: Asabiyyah: The Theory of Social Cohesion
content_en:
The concept of asabiyyah (social solidarity, group feeling, or tribal cohesion) is the central explanatory mechanism of Ibn Khaldun's theory of history and politics. Understanding it is essential for understanding Al-Muqaddimah as a whole.

Asabiyyah refers to the intense group loyalty and mutual solidarity that binds members of a clan, tribe, or similar social unit together. It is not merely sentimental attachment but a functional force: asabiyyah motivates members of a group to defend one another, to sacrifice for the collective good, to follow a common leader, and to compete successfully against other groups. Without strong asabiyyah, Ibn Khaldun argued, no group can achieve political power or maintain it.

Ibn Khaldun's foundational observation was that strong asabiyyah is characteristically found among peoples of the desert and frontier — nomads, Bedouin, mountain dwellers — who live in austere conditions that demand mutual dependence and harden the character. Settled, urbanized peoples, by contrast, gradually lose their asabiyyah as the comforts and individualism of city life dissolve the bonds of solidarity.

This differential in asabiyyah explains, in Ibn Khaldun's framework, the cyclical pattern of historical change: desert peoples with strong asabiyyah periodically overwhelm and replace settled ruling groups whose asabiyyah has decayed. The new rulers then settle, become urbanized, and begin the same cycle of decline. Ibn Khaldun saw this pattern repeating across the history of the Islamic world — from the Arab conquest of the Persian and Byzantine empires to the successive waves of Berber, Turkish, and Mongol conquest.

The concept also explains the role of religion and prophecy in history: a prophet who brings a genuine divine message achieves an asabiyyah of a different and superior kind — rooted in shared spiritual conviction rather than merely tribal loyalty — which explains why the early Islamic community, despite being a small group in Arabia, was able to achieve such extraordinary military and political success. Religion amplifies and transforms asabiyyah into a force capable of uniting diverse groups under a common cause.
